title: Cyanea angustifolia
taxonomy: Cyanea angustifolia is a species of flowering plant in the family Goodeniaceae. It is native to Hawaii and is an endangered species due to habitat loss and degradation. As a member of the Goodeniaceae family, Cyanea angustifolia is closely related to other plants in the same family, such as Scaevola and Goodenia. The Goodeniaceae family is a group of flowering plants that are primarily found in Australia and the Pacific Islands. The classification of Cyanea angustifolia reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary history.

intro: Cyanea angustifolia, also known as the 'nene head' or 'Hawaiian blue hibiscus', is a rare and endangered flowering plant species endemic to the Hawaiian Islands. The plant has been traditionally used in Hawaiian medicine to treat various health conditions, including wounds, skin irritations, and respiratory issues, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which are attributed to its bioactive constituents, including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins. The bioactive constituents of Cyanea angustifolia have been shown to exhibit antioxidant, antiviral, and antifungal activities, contributing to its potential health benefits. In traditional Hawaiian medicine, the plant is often prepared as a tea or infusion, or used topically as a poultice, and is also sometimes incorporated into herbal salves, ointments, and creams for its wound-healing and skin-soothing properties.
